Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, but honestly it was hard with my situation. I'd usually set aside about three hours a day for studying, mostly in the evenings when the house was quieter. I'd read through the materials and take notes by hand since that helps me remember things better. Sometimes I'd batch my study sessions on weekends when I could really focus without interruptions.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to study regularly, but when deadlines approached I'd shift into a more intense mode. Some weeks I barely touched the course because other responsibilities came up, then I'd have to catch up all at once. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check if there were new assignments posted or to see announcements from the instructors. Sometimes my peer study group would message me about something on the platform, and that would prompt me to go check it out. I also logged in when I needed to download reading materials for offline study since my internet isn't always reliable.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the LMS in the morning became kind of automatic for me. The peer support group I was connected with really helped with that because they'd post reminders and we'd hold each other accountable. It felt less like a chore when I knew other people were expecting me to show up and participate in discussions.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me since that matches how I learn best. I'd download PDFs and articles, then annotate them heavily. The discussion forums were also helpful because I could see how other students understood the concepts, and sometimes their explanations clicked better than the official materials.
Q6: I often skipped the video lectures or only watched them partially. With my limited data, streaming videos was expensive and time-consuming. I figured I could get the same information from the written transcripts or readings, so I prioritized those instead. Some of the optional quizzes I also ignored because they didn't count toward my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. I also valued anything that helped me understand difficult concepts more clearly. If I could see how something would improve my grade or deepen my understanding, I'd invest the time. But if it felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was no pressure attached to them. I'd only do optional stuff if I was genuinely curious or if my study group recommended it. Required activities had this weight to them, like I knew I had to complete them no matter what, so I prioritized those even when I was tired or bored.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline actually. I'd download everything I could and then study from printed notes or saved files on my phone. I also used a few free textbooks I found online and some YouTube videos when I had access to wifi at the library. The LMS was more like a starting point than my main study tool.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading printed materials, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also study with classmates in person, quizzing each other and explaining concepts, but the LMS had no idea that was happening. My best learning often happened away from the screen.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a reading or resource once, download it immediately, and then never open it on the platform again. I'd study that material for days or even weeks offline. So my LMS activity looked minimal even though I was engaging deeply with the content on my own terms.
Q12: There were stretches where I just felt bored with the material, like it wasn't challenging enough or it felt repetitive. When that happened, I'd lose momentum and my attendance would slip. Also, when things got financially tight at home, my mind would wander during study time and I couldn't focus properly on the course.
Q13: Definitely. When I had too many assignments across different courses, I'd have to triage and the LMS activities for this course sometimes got pushed aside. Fatigue was real too since I'd be so tired some evenings that I just couldn't bring myself to log in. I'd tell myself I'd catch up on the weekend instead.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My eyes would move across the text but my brain was somewhere else entirely. Those sessions felt like going through the motions just to say I'd done something. I'd have to go back later and actually engage with the content properly.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they felt tedious even though I knew participation mattered. Sometimes I avoided starting assignments because I felt intimidated by them and didn't know where to begin. Once I actually started, it was usually fine, but that initial resistance was hard to overcome.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. I'd check the LMS more frequently as deadlines approached and my activity would spike. Without a deadline looming, I'd sometimes drift and not log in for days. It's not the healthiest pattern, but the structure of deadlines kept me accountable when my own motivation dipped.
Q17: When I got feedback on assignments, it definitely motivated me to engage more with similar content. Positive feedback made me feel like my efforts were paying off, so I'd push harder. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd feel disconnected from the course and less motivated to put in extra effort.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my time, even if I didn't always follow through perfectly. Having everything organized in the LMS made it easier to know what was expected. But the structure also felt rigid sometimes, like it didn't account for students who learn differently or have unpredictable schedules.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement much easier for me given my internet situation. The peer support component was also great because it gave me a community to lean on. What made it harder was when resources were only available as streaming content or when there were too many small tasks that felt fragmented.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need, trying to understand the platform, or maybe they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. They could also be dealing with personal issues that make it hard to follow through even when they're trying. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I performed reasonably well but my LMS activity was probably lower than expected because I studied so much offline. If you know how to learn efficiently and you use external resources, you don't need to be constantly logged in. The grade reflects the learning, not the clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and which resources you access, which is useful for identifying students who might be struggling. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and the mental effort someone puts in. They also can't capture whether someone is genuinely learning or just going through motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections to other things I know. For me, engagement is internal, it's about my mind being active with the content even if my LMS activity looks passive.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on my peer support group for motivation and accountability. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes or the discussions I had with classmates outside the platform. It also can't show that even when I felt bored, I pushed through because I'm determined to succeed despite my circumstances.
